perturbation.original_text: has been convicted of a felony or pleaded nolo contendere to a felony charge or, within the ten year period immediately preceding the application for registration, has been convicted of or pleaded nolo contendere to a misdemeanor charge or has been the subject of a civil action alleging: violation of a franchise; anti-fraud or securities law; fraud; embezzlement; fraudulent conversion or misappropriation of property; unfair or deceptive practices; or comparable allegations.
perturbation.changed_text: has been convicted of a serious crime or pleaded nolo contendere to a serious crime charge or, within the ten year period immediately preceding the application for registration, has been convicted of or pleaded nolo contendere to a less serious crime charge or has been the subject of a civil action alleging: violation of a franchise; anti-fraud or securities law; fraud; embezzlement; fraudulent conversion or misappropriation of property; unfair or deceptive practices; or comparable allegations.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'felony' and 'misdemeanor' are replaced with 'serious crime' and 'less serious crime.' This introduces ambiguity as the legal definitions of felony and misdemeanor are well-established, while the alternative phrasing is open to interpretation. The legal context regarding disclosure requirements is thereby weakened.
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Business Law Section 683(1)(a) specifically requires disclosure of prior felony convictions of the franchisor or its principals. Replacing 'felony' with the vague term 'serious crime' obscures this requirement and potentially allows franchisors to avoid disclosing information that would otherwise be required, thereby contradicting the intent and letter of the law.

2025-05-30 2025-06-13 Share Facebook Email SECTION 683 Disclosure requirements General Business (GBS) CHAPTER 20, ARTICLE 33 § 683. Disclosure requirements. 1. It shall be unlawful and prohibited for any person to offer to sell or sell in this state any franchise unless and until there shall have been registered with the department of law, prior to such offer or sale, a written statement to be known as an "offering prospectus" concerning the contemplated offer or sale, which shall contain the information and representations set forth in and required by this section. Any uniform disclosure document approved for use by any agency of the federal government or sister state may be utilized and sought to be registered, provided that said uniform disclosure documents comply with the provisions of this article. 2. The offering prospectus sought to be registered with the department of law shall be filed with the department, accompanied by an application for registration on forms prescribed by the department, and shall contain the following: (a) The name of the franchisor, the name under which the franchisor is doing or intends to do business, and the name of any parent or affiliated company that will engage in business transactions with franchisees. (b) The franchisor's principal business address and the name and address of its agent in this state authorized to receive process. (c) The business form of the franchisor, whether corporate, partnership, or otherwise. (d) Such information concerning the identity and business experience of persons affiliated with the franchisor as the department of law may by rule prescribe. (e) A statement as to whether the franchisor and its principals, officers, partners, directors, or any other person identified in the application for registration: (1) Has been convicted of a felony, or pleaded nolo contendere to a felony charge, or held liable or enjoined in a civil action by a final judgment if such civil action involved fraud, embezzlement, fraudulent conversion or misappropriation of property
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts New York General Business Law Section 683(1)(a), as explained by the LLM, because it replaces the legally defined terms 'felony' and 'misdemeanor' with the ambiguous terms 'serious crime' and 'less serious crime'. Scraped Law Snippet 1, which contains SECTION 683, states that the offering prospectus should contain a statement as to whether the franchisor and its principals have been convicted of a felony, or pleaded nolo contendere to a felony charge. By using the altered terminology, the changed text weakens the disclosure requirements, potentially allowing franchisors to avoid disclosing information that the law requires, thus making it more difficult to enforce the law and comply with regulatory requirements.

perturbation.original_text: filed as debtor (or had filed against it) a petition to start an action under the U.S. Bankruptcy Code (or any comparable foreign law); (b) obtained a discharge of its debts under the bankruptcy code
perturbation.changed_text: filed as debtor (or had filed against it) a petition to start an action related to insolvency (or any comparable foreign law); (b) resolved their debts
perturbation.explanation: The phrase 'under the U.S. Bankruptcy Code' is replaced with the more general 'related to insolvency,' and 'obtained a discharge of its debts under the bankruptcy code' is replaced with 'resolved their debts'. These replacements make it difficult to assess the franchisor's financial standing, and 'resolved their debts' is too vague. This change introduces ambiguity and legal uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The U.S. Bankruptcy Code uses specific terms like 'debtor' and 'discharge'. By using the vaguer terms 'related to insolvency' and 'resolved their debts,' the contract becomes ambiguous and potentially misleading, failing to provide the clear financial history required by franchise disclosure laws and undermining the protections offered by federal bankruptcy law. The modified text lacks the defined legal meaning associated with 'bankruptcy' under 11 U.S.C. § 101.

Definitions In this title the following definitions shall apply: (1) The term "accountant" means accountant authorized under applicable law to practice public accounting, and includes professional accounting association, corporation, or partnership, if so authorized. (2) The term "affiliate" means- (A) entity that directly or indirectly owns, controls, or holds with power to vote, 20 percent or more of the outstanding voting securities of t
perturbation.justification: The original text specifically mentions 'U.S. Bankruptcy Code' and 'discharge of debts under the bankruptcy code,' terms that have defined legal meaning under 11 U.S.C. § 101, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, which provides definitions within the Bankruptcy Code. By replacing these with the vaguer terms 'related to insolvency' and 'resolved their debts,' the changed text introduces ambiguity and weakens the clarity of the financial disclosures. This deviation from established legal terminology undermines the enforceability of the clause and potentially misaligns with regulatory requirements for clear financial history, thus creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: is subject to a currently effective injunctive or restrictive order or decree relating to the franchise, or under a Federal, State or Canadian franchise, securities, antitrust, trade regulation or trade practice law, resulting from a concluded or pending action or proceeding brought by a public agency; or is subject to any currently effective order of any national securities association or national securities exchange, as defined in the Securities and Exchange Act of 1934, suspending or expelling such person from membership in such association or exchange
perturbation.changed_text: is subject to a currently effective court order relating to the franchise, or under a Federal, State or Canadian business law, resulting from a concluded or pending action or proceeding brought by a government entity; or is subject to any currently effective order of any national business association or national business exchange, suspending or expelling such person from membership in such association or exchange
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces specific legal terms such as 'injunctive or restrictive order or decree relating to ... securities, antitrust, trade regulation or trade practice law' with more generic terms like 'court order relating to... business law'. It also swaps 'public agency' for 'government entity' and 'national securities association or national securities exchange, as defined in the Securities and Exchange Act of 1934' with 'national business association or national business exchange'. These substitutions weaken the specific legal obligations to which the franchisor is bound, leading to a less transparent and potentially misleading disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Securities Exchange Act of 1934 defines specific terms and regulations for securities associations and exchanges. By using the broader term 'national business association or national business exchange,' the modified contract dilutes the legal accountability associated with actions by securities regulators. This contradiction allows for potentially misleading disclosures and undermines the investor protection goals of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934.

Definitions and application (a) Definitions When used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires- (1) The term "exchange" means any organization, association, or group of persons, whether incorporated or unincorporated, which constitutes, maintains, or provides a market place or facilities for bringing together purchasers and sellers of securities or for otherwise performing wi
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: U.S.C. Title 15 - COMMERCE AND TRADE 15 U.S.C. United States Code, 2011 Edition Title 15 - COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 2B - SECURITIES EXCHANGES Sec. 78c - Definitions and application From the U.S. Government Publishing Office, www.gpo.gov §78c. Definitions and application (a) Definitions When used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires— (1) The term “exchange” means any organization, association, or group of persons, whether incorporated or unincorporated, which constitutes, maintains, or provides a market place or facilities for bringing together purchasers and sellers of securities or for otherwise performing with respect to securities the functions commonly performed by a stock exchange as that term is generally understood, and includes the market place and the market facilities maintained by such exchange. (2) The term “facility” when used with respect to an exchange includes its premises, tangible or intangible property whether on the premises or not, any right to the use of such premises or property or any service thereof for the purpose of effecting or reporting a transaction on an exchange (including, among other things, any system of communication to or from the exchange, by ticker or otherwise, maintained by or with the consent of the exchange), and any right of the exchange to the use of any property or service. (3)(A) The term “member” when used with respect to a national securities exchange means (i) any natural person permitted to effect transactions on the floor of the exchange without the services of another person acting as broker, (ii) any registered broker or dealer with which such a natural person is associated, (iii) any registered broker or dealer permitted to designate as a representative such a natural person, and (iv) any other registered broker or dealer which agrees to be regulated by such exchange and with respect to which the exchange undertakes to enforce compli ance with the provisions of this chapter, the rules and regulations thereu
perturbation.justification: The modified text replaces specific legal terms related to securities with broader, less precise terms like 'national business association or national business exchange.' Snippet 1, citing 15 USC 78c, defines 'exchange' in the context of securities, highlighting the importance of specific regulatory language in securities law. The change weakens the obligation's force, potentially undermining investor protection goals and diluting legal accountability as described in the law explanation.
